facts.text: The word 'candy' entered English from the Old French 'çucre candi' (sugar candy), which has roots in the Arabic 'qandi', Persian 'qand', and Sanskrit 'khanda' — all words for sugar.
Sugarcane is indigenous to tropical South and Southeast Asia; ancient Indians boiled sugarcane juice into pieces called khanda, an early form of sugar candy.
Before sugar was readily available, candy was based on honey, which was used in Ancient China, the Middle East, Egypt, Greece and the Roman Empire to coat fruits and flowers to preserve them.
Before the Industrial Revolution, candy was often considered a form of medicine, used to calm the digestive system or soothe a sore throat.
In the Middle Ages, a candy called 'chamber spice' was made with cloves, ginger, aniseed, juniper berries, almonds and pine kernels dipped in melted sugar.
The first candy came to America during the early 18th century from Britain and France; even the simplest form — rock candy — was considered a luxury.
In 1847, the invention of the candy press (also called a 'toy machine') made it possible to produce multiple shapes and sizes of candy at once.
In 1851, confectioners began using a revolving steam pan to assist in boiling sugar, eliminating the need to continuously stir and distributing heat more evenly.

Early industrial-era manufacturers produced bright candy colors using hazardous substances: green from chromium(III) oxide and copper acetate, red from lead oxide and mercury sulfide, yellow from lead chromate, and white from chalk or arsenic trioxide.
By 1906, research into candy additives and food industry exposés led to the passage of the Pure Food and Drug Act, the first federal US law to regulate food and drugs including candy.
Sugar candies can be classified as noncrystalline (hard candies, caramels, toffees, nougats) or crystalline (fondant, fudge), based on microscopic crystal structure rather than visible appearance.
When sugar syrup is heated to 171°C (340°F) or higher, sucrose molecules break down into simpler sugars, creating an amber-colored substance known as caramel.

Candy recipes cannot be copyrighted or patented effectively, so the industry relies significantly on trade secret protection; seemingly minor differences in machinery, temperature, or timing cause noticeable differences in the final product.
Foil and cellophane were imported to the US from France by DuPont in 1925; prior to 1914, candy was commonly sold unwrapped from street carts where it was exposed to dirt and insects.

Because of its high sugar concentration, bacteria are not usually able to grow in candy, giving it a longer shelf life than many other foods; hard candy may last indefinitely in good storage conditions.
Global sales of candies were estimated at approximately US$118 billion in 2012; in the US, $2 is spent on chocolate for every $1 spent on non-chocolate candy.

A 1959 Swedish dental health campaign started the tradition of 'lördagsgodis' (Saturday candy), encouraging people to limit candy to once a week; as a result, Sweden has become the world's largest per-capita candy consumer since at least 2009.

The link between candy and dental caries was formally identified through the Vipeholm experiments, in which intellectually disabled people were fed large amounts of candy; the experiments are today considered to have violated multiple principles of medical ethics.

Penny candy became the first material good that children in America spent their own money on, driving a child-focused candy market that grew dramatically after the 1830s industrial revolution in candy-making.

Some candies, including marshmallows and gummy bears, contain gelatin derived from animal collagen, making them unsuitable for vegans and many vegetarians; alternatives include agar, pectin, starch and gum arabic.

title: Chocolate
summary: Chocolate is made from cacao tree seeds with origins in Mesoamerica. The Mayans and Aztecs considered cacao a gift from the gods; Spanish conquistadors introduced it to Europe in the 16th century.

facts.text: Chocolate is made from roasted and ground cocoa beans — the processed seeds of the cacao tree (Theobroma cacao).
Evidence for domestication of the cacao tree exists as early as 5300 BP in South America, by the Mayo-Chinchipe culture in present-day southeast Ecuador.
The word 'chocolate' is a Spanish loanword, first recorded in English in 1604 and in Spanish in 1579.
Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés may have been the first European to encounter chocolate, observing it at the court of Moctezuma II in 1520.
White chocolate is made with cocoa butter but without cocoa solids.
Cacao was considered a gift from the gods by the Mayans and the Aztecs, and the cocoa bean was used as currency across civilizations.
The first official recording of a shipment of chocolate to Europe was 1585.
Chocolate in Mesoamerica was a bitter drink flavored with vanilla, earflower, and chili, capped with dark brown foam created by pouring between containers from a height.

title: Gummy Bear
summary: Gummy bears are small fruit gum candies invented in Germany by Hans Riegel Sr. of Haribo in 1922. Traditional recipe uses sugar, glucose syrup, starch, flavoring, food coloring, citric acid, and gelatin.

facts.text: Gummy bears were invented in Germany by Hans Riegel Sr. of Haribo in 1922, inspired by trained bears seen at European street festivals.
Hans Riegel Sr. started the Haribo company in 1920 in Bonn, Germany.
The original gummy bear was called the Dancing Bear (Tanzbär); the current Gold-Bear (Goldbär) form was introduced in 1967.
Traditional gummy bears are made from sugar, glucose syrup, starch, flavoring, food coloring, citric acid, and gelatin.
Gummy bear production uses a specialized machine called a starch mogul, which stamps the bear image into powdered starch molds.
The name 'gummy' comes from gum arabic, the original base ingredient used to produce the candies.
In the United States, Haribo gummy bears are sold in five flavors: raspberry (red), orange (orange), strawberry (green), pineapple (colorless), and lemon (yellow).

title: Hard Candy
summary: Hard candy (or boiled sweet) is prepared from sugar-based syrups heated to 160°C, resulting in a non-crystalline, amorphous product that is nearly 100% sugar by weight.

facts.text: Hard candy is prepared from sugar-based syrups heated to a temperature of 160°C (320°F).
Most hard candy is nearly 100% sugar by weight with negligible water content in the final product.
Hard candies are non-crystalline, amorphous candies containing about 98% or more solid sugar.
Japanese hard candies are known as bekkō ame (鼈甲飴), meaning 'tortoiseshell candy'.
Hard candies are historically associated with cough drops; apothecaries used sugar candy to make prescriptions more palatable.
Hard candy varieties include candy cane, lollipops, rock, aniseed twists, and bêtises de Cambrai.
Sugar-free hard candies use isomalt as a sugar substitute, often sweetened further with aspartame, sucralose, saccharin, or xylitol.
